title: TabR: Tabular Deep Learning Meets Nearest Neighbors in 2023
authors: Yury Gorishniy, Ivan Rubachev, Nikolay Kartashev, Daniil Shlenskii, Akim Kotelnikov, Artem Babenko (Yandex Research)
tokenization_method: Retrieval-augmented, OBJECT-level (not per-feature) representation. An Input Module first normalizes/one-hot-encodes features into one flat vector per row; a feed-forward encoder E maps it to a single object embedding x~ in R^d. The novelty is NOT a feature tokenizer but a custom kNN-like retrieval module R inserted as a residual branch after E: for a target object, R encodes a set of candidate training objects with the SAME encoder, computes similarities, takes the top-m most similar, and aggregates their (key-difference + label) values via softmax-weighted attention. So 'tokens' here are whole training EXAMPLES (rows), and attention is over the instance axis, not the feature axis.
feature_types_handled: numerical, binary, categorical (mixed). Categoricals are one-hot encoded inside the Input Module (entity-style learned embeddings optional); the full-power TabR variant can additionally apply PLR/periodic numerical feature embeddings from Gorishniy et al. 2022. No native missing-value handling; no built-in time-series mechanism (temporal use requires manual constraint that retrieval only sees the past — see limitations).
token_granularity: per-object (per training example / row). One token = one encoded object x~ in R^d. The retrieval context is a set of m such object-tokens (default m=96). This is orthogonal to per-feature tokenization: TabR does not produce one token per feature.
numerical_strategy: raw normalized scalars in the simple variant (TabR-S, linear encoder, no feature embeddings); the full TabR optionally uses PLR/periodic numerical embeddings (Gorishniy et al. 2022) in the Input Module. The paper explicitly treats numerical-feature embeddings and retrieval as two SEPARATE, partially-overlapping power sources.
categorical_strategy: one-hot encoding (and/or learned embeddings) in the Input Module, then flattened into the object vector before encoder E. No special compositional or target-aware categorical scheme is the contribution.
high_cardinality_handling: none specific. Categoricals go through one-hot/learned-embedding in the Input Module; no hashing, no target encoding, no shared codebooks. High-cardinality fields are not a focus and would inflate the input vector / embedding tables as in any FT-style model.
temporal_handling: no built-in temporal mechanism, but the paper is unusually explicit (Appendix B) that for time series the retrieval MUST be constrained to the past, and that 'past' may need windowing to exclude too-old/too-recent data. Fusion of time-varying vs static features is left to the Input Module flattening; there is no patching/recurrence/channel-token.
scales_to_many_features: Feature-count scaling is a NON-issue for TabR's core, which is its key distinction from FT-Transformer-style models: attention is over CANDIDATE OBJECTS (rows), so cost is O(N_candidates * d), NOT O(k^2) in feature count k. Benchmark feature counts are modest (6 to ~136). The real scaling pressure is the SAMPLE axis: with Icand = Itrain (default), each step re-encodes/searches all training objects, so TabR 'may not scale to truly large datasets as-is' (Appendix B). Mitigations: subsection 5.1 'context freeze' (mine contexts once after N epochs, then reuse) and using a candidate subset; evaluated up to ~3M-object Weather. NOT validated for very wide (70+ heterogeneous, high-cardinality) tables specifically.
downstream_model: feed-forward network (MLP-style encoder E with N_E blocks + retrieval module R as residual branch + predictor P with N_P blocks). TabR-S uses a linear encoder (N_E=0) and one predictor block. ~simple parametric backbone, NOT a Transformer stack over feature tokens.
pretraining_objective: none; trained fully supervised end-to-end. The retrieval signal (neighbor labels) is exploited at train and inference time, but there is no self-supervised pretraining and no cross-table transfer objective.
key_results: On the default benchmark (11 datasets, 6.4K-3M objects): TabR has the best average rank among tabular DL models and sets new SOTA on several datasets. The full TabR outperforms MLP-PLR (the strongest prior parametric DL model) on CA, OT, BL, WE, CO and ties elsewhere except MI. Headline: on the Grinsztajn et al. 2022 'why tree models still outperform DL' benchmark, TabR beats XGBoost/CatBoost on the small-to-middle-scale tasks originally used to show GBDT superiority. Step ablation (Table 2): improvements over MLP only appear at Step-2 (key-only L2 similarity); removing ANY of {context labels, key-only representation, L2 distance} collapses performance back to MLP level.
baselines_compared: Retrieval-based: kNN, DNNR, NPT (closest deep competitor; TabR is dramatically faster), DKL/SAINT-style, ANP/Neural-Process variants. Parametric DL: MLP, MLP-PLR, ResNet, FT-Transformer. GBDT: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost (with ensembling for fairness).
transferable_idea: Move the attention/'tokenization' from the FEATURE axis to the INSTANCE axis: represent each row as one object-token and let a kNN-like retrieval module attend over training examples. The single most reusable detail is the Step-2 similarity: a KEY-ONLY (no separate query), L2-distance similarity S = -||W_K(x~) - W_K(x~_i)||^2 paired with a value V = W_Y(y_i) + T(W_K(x~) - W_K(x~_i)) that adds the neighbor's LABEL embedding plus a learned key-difference correction. For the 70+-feature setting this is attractive precisely because its cost is independent of feature count -- it sidesteps the O(k^2) wall that per-feature tokenizers hit.
limitations: Authors-stated (Appendix B): (1) using real training rows for predictions may be legally/ethically/business-inappropriate; (2) candidate set may need application-specific dynamic filtering; (3) retrieval at train time MUST mirror deployment -- for time series, retrieve only from the (windowed) past, and avoid leaking 'related objects' (e.g. same-query documents in ranking) unavailable at inference; (4) retrieval overhead means TabR 'may not scale to truly large datasets as-is'. Implied: not a per-feature tokenizer (so it does NOT directly solve heterogeneous-feature TOKENIZATION), no high-cardinality compression, no missing-value story, supervised-only.
pitfalls: 1) TabR is filed under tabular-transformers but it is NOT a feature-token Transformer -- its attention is over training objects, not features. Do not cite it as a feature-tokenization advance; its relevance to THIS topic is as an alternative that makes feature-count scaling irrelevant, not as a tokenizer. 2) The 'beats GBDT' headline is on Grinsztajn small-to-middle-scale tasks; the authors themselves note tree models 'remain a cheaper solution' and retrieval 'is not universally beneficial for all datasets' (Table 3 has datasets where it does not help). 3) Train/deploy retrieval mismatch is a real correctness hazard, not a nuance -- for temporal/static-mixed data (the topic's setting) naive retrieval leaks the future. 4) Default Icand=Itrain re-searches all training data every step; the strong numbers assume this, while the cheaper 'context freeze' trades some accuracy. 5) Numerical-embedding power (PLR) and retrieval power are entangled but not orthogonal -- ablations show neither fully recovers the other, so claimed gains depend on which is present.
relevance_to_topic: A high-value CONTRAST point: it shows a way to get GBDT-beating tabular DL that is, by construction, immune to the feature-count blow-up that plagues per-feature tokenizers -- but it does so by punting on feature tokenization entirely and attacking the instance axis instead, while introducing a train/deploy retrieval-leakage hazard that is acute for the topic's mixed temporal+static, 70+-feature target.
code_available: yes -- official PyTorch repo https://github.com/yandex-research/tabular-dl-tabr (MIT license; supports numerical/binary/categorical features and optional PLR embeddings; reproduction notebooks + TOML configs; lightly maintained, ~10 commits).
